eli3: Ad click prediction estimates the probability a user will click an ad (CTR = click-through rate). Google Ads, Facebook Ads, and programmatic platforms use this to decide which ad to show and how much to charge.
Real-time bidding happens in milliseconds: user visits page → auction triggered → bids calculated → winner selected → ad displayed. All in <100ms. Billions of auctions daily.
Unique challenges: sparse categorical features (billions of users, queries, ads), real-time freshness (trending topics), and auction dynamics (higher predicted CTR = higher bid value).

whenWhy.title: Search advertising
whenWhy.body: Google Ads: predict CTR for each query-ad pair. Determines ad rank (bid × quality score = expected value). Higher CTR prediction means lower cost per click for advertiser. Revenue optimization for search engine.

whenWhy.title: Display and social advertising
whenWhy.body: Facebook, Instagram, display networks: predict CTR based on user demographics, interests, behavior. Targeting: show relevant ads to right users. Frequency capping: don't show same ad too often.

whenWhy.title: Programmatic real-time bidding (RTB)
whenWhy.body: Ad exchanges: DSPs (Demand Side Platforms) bid on impressions in real-time. Predict CTR, conversion rate to determine bid price. Latency critical: must respond in <100ms or lose auction.

whenWhy.title: Retargeting and personalization
whenWhy.body: Predict likelihood to convert based on site visits, cart abandonment. Dynamic creative optimization: which ad creative performs best for this user. Cross-device attribution: user journey across phone, tablet, desktop.

deepDiveIntro: CTR prediction features: (1) User: demographics, interests, behavior history. (2) Query/context: search terms, page content, app category. (3) Ad: advertiser, creative, landing page, keywords. (4) Cross features: user-ad interactions, query-ad relevance. All high-cardinality categorical (one-hot encoding infeasible).

Models: Logistic regression baseline (scalable, interpretable). Factorization machines: capture feature interactions. Field-aware factorization (FFM): different embeddings per field. Deep learning: Wide & Deep, DeepFM, xDeepFM for feature interactions. Embeddings for categorical features, dense layers for combinations.

deepDive.heading: Feature Engineering
deepDive.body: Categorical: user_id (billions), query_id (millions), ad_id (millions), device, location. High cardinality → embeddings. Cross features: user × ad (co-occurrence), query × ad (relevance). Continuous: time of day, historical CTR for this ad. Sequence: user recent queries, browsing history (transformer/RNN). Freshness: trending queries, new ads.

deepDive.heading: Model Evolution
deepDive.body: Logistic regression: sparse features, L1 regularization, online learning via FTRL. Factorization machines: pairwise feature interactions via latent vectors. Field-aware FM: separate embeddings per field for each feature. Deep: Wide & Deep (memorization + generalization). DeepFM: FM + deep neural network. xDeepFM: explicit high-order feature interactions via CIN.

deepDive.heading: Online Learning
deepDive.body: Ad patterns change fast (trending topics, new ads). FTRL-Proximal: per-coordinate learning rates, L1 regularization for sparsity. Updates model continuously from click stream. Fast adaptation critical. Downside: model instability, need safeguards (outlier detection, gradient clipping).

deepDive.heading: Auction Mechanics
deepDive.body: Generalized Second Price (GSP): winner pays price of second-highest bidder. Ad rank: bid × quality_score (CTR prediction). Expected revenue: CTR × bid. Optimization: VCG (Vickrey-Clarke-Groves) for truthfulness but complex. Reserve prices: minimum bid to enter auction. Reserve optimization based on competition.

deepDive.heading: Serving at Scale
deepDive.body: Feature store: sub-10ms lookup for user/ad features. Model serving: TensorFlow Serving, custom C++ servers. Latency budget: <50ms for prediction. Batching: score multiple ads in parallel. Caching: popular query-ad pairs. A/B testing: measure CTR, revenue, advertiser satisfaction.

takeaways: CTR prediction: estimate click probability for ad auctions.
Sparse categorical features require embeddings, high cardinality.
Models: logistic regression, factorization machines, deep learning.
Online learning: FTRL for real-time adaptation to trends.
Auction: bid × quality_score determines rank and price.

edgeCases.q: How do you handle new ads (cold start)?
edgeCases.a: New ads have no click history. Solutions: (1) Content features: advertiser, landing page category, creative attributes. (2) Similar ads: find similar existing ads, borrow CTR. (3) Exploration: show new ads to learn CTR (bandit approach). (4) Bayesian: start with prior (category average), update with observations. (5) Advertiser history: new ad from trusted advertiser gets benefit of doubt.

Competitors click to exhaust budget, bots generate fake clicks. Detection: (1) IP analysis: too many clicks from same IP. (2) Behavioral: click patterns (time on site after click). (3) Device fingerprinting: identify bots. (4) Rate limiting: cap clicks per user per time. (5) Post-click quality: bounce rate, conversion. Refund invalid clicks to advertisers.

edgeCases.q: How do you balance revenue vs user experience?
edgeCases.a: Tension: more ads = more revenue, but annoys users. Solutions: (1) Quality score: low-quality ads (high bounce rate) penalized even with high bids. (2) Ad load optimization: test number of ads per page. (3) Relevance: prioritize CTR over bid for user satisfaction. (4) Format: native ads less intrusive. (5) Long-term: measure user retention, not just immediate clicks.

sources.label: McMahan et al. — Ad Click Prediction: A View from the Trenches
Cheng et al. — Wide & Deep Learning for Recommender Systems
